draft.introduction: The Shanti Parva (Book of Peace) is the twelfth book of the Mahabharata, containing Bhishma’s deathbed teachings on dharma, governance, and liberation. It is the epic’s most extensive philosophical discourse.

translation.text: After the great battle was over and the Pandavas had won the kingdom, Yudhishthira was overcome with grief at the carnage. He said, ‘I have killed my kinsmen, my teachers, my grandsire. What kingdom is worth this slaughter? I wish to renounce all and enter the forest.’ Then the sages and Krishna counseled him to accept the burden of righteous rule.

translator_notes.note: Yudhishthira’s moral crisis after victory mirrors Arjuna’s crisis before battle in the Bhagavad Gita, creating a frame around the entire war narrative. The Shanti Parva’s answer—that righteous governance is itself a form of dharma—integrates the active and contemplative ideals.
